title: High-fidelity entangling gates and nonlocal circuits with neutral atoms
authors: Simon J. Evered, Muqing Xu, Sophie H. Li, Alexandra A. Geim, J. Pablo Bonilla Ataides, Marcin Kalinowski, Dolev Bluvstein, Nishad Maskara, Christian Kokail, Markus Greiner, Vladan Vuletić, Mikhail D. Lukin
abstract: Creation and manipulation of entanglement with low error is essential in quantum information systems. In practice, two-qubit entangling gates constitute a dominant error source, limiting circuit depths and performance in fault-tolerant architectures. Using a neutral-atom quantum processor, we realize entangling CZ gates with a high Rabi frequency smooth-amplitude pulse, employing state-selective readout and qubit reuse for fast calibration, and achieve state-of-the-art fidelities of 99.854(4)% which improve to 99.941(3)% upon loss postselection, with stable performance for 10 hours. We then use these low-error gates in quantum circuits with coherent atom rearrangement. We first benchmark performance by creating and disentangling cluster states, and subsequently implement scrambling circuits featuring longer-range connectivity to study non-locally entangled states generated through chaotic dynamics. These results pave the way towards deep-circuit, efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation.
summary: This paper reports a significant advancement in neutral-atom quantum computing by demonstrating extremely high-fidelity CZ gates. By using optimized pulse shapes and efficient atom rearrangement, the researchers achieved gate fidelities near 99.9%. They further demonstrated that these high-fidelity gates can be used to execute complex, nonlocal quantum circuits that exhibit super-ballistic scrambling. This progress provides a scalable path toward fault-tolerant quantum error correction.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving high-fidelity two-qubit entangling gates and implementing complex, nonlocal quantum circuits to enable fault-tolerant quantum computation.
main_result: The researchers achieved state-of-the-art CZ gate fidelities of 99.854(4)% (improving to 99.941(3)% with loss postselection) and successfully implemented nonlocal circuits exhibiting super-ballistic scrambling.
method: The team used a neutral-atom processor with smooth-amplitude pulses, randomized benchmarking (RB), and symmetric stabilizer benchmarking (SSB) to characterize gate performance and error sources.
model_or_system: A reconfigurable neutral-atom quantum processor using 87Rb atoms trapped in optical tweezers (SLM and AOD-controlled) and manipulated via Rydberg blockade interactions.
key_observables: CZ gate fidelity, return probability, cross-entropy benchmarking (XEB), entanglement entropy, and Porter-Thomas distribution convergence.
important_parameters: Rabi frequency of 2pi x 17 MHz, Rydberg state n=53, gate stability over 10 hours, and atom loss rate per gate.
main_assumptions: The error analysis assumes that the primary error drivers are Rydberg state decay, intermediate state scattering, and position-induced frequency noise.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the gate pulse profile and processor architecture; Figure 2 presents benchmarking results and fidelity stability; Figure 3 details the error budget; Figure 4 illustrates gates with moving atoms; Figure 5 demonstrates 20-qubit circuit performance and XEB results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the gate design and architecture, presents high-fidelity benchmarking and error analysis, demonstrates stability and robustness to atom movement, and concludes with the implementation of complex nonlocal scrambling circuits.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and many-body dynamics as it demonstrates the control of entanglement in large-scale, reconfigurable systems and explores the transition to chaotic, scrambling dynamics in a programmable platform.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. CZ entangling gates in a neutral atom processor with fast cycle rates. (A) Rydberg gate realized through a smooth-amplitude gate pulse at roughly Ω= 2π × 17 MHz peak Rabi frequency, with a detuning profile δ(t). Two-photon excitation scheme consists of transitions at 420 nm and 1015 nm, exciting from qubit state |1⟩to the Rydberg state |r⟩= 53S1/2, mJ = −1/2 through the intermediate state manifold |e⟩= 6P3/2 with intermediate state detuning ∆= 2π × 7.8 GHz (see fig. S1B for more detailed excitation diagram). (B) Images of the atoms separated into processor zones, with gate sites used for benchmarking and high-fidelity quantum circuits, and state-selective readout to perform loss...

FIG. 2. Stable low-error CZ gate performance. (A) Benchmarking methods based on repeated CZ gate applications: global echo randomized benchmarking (“echo RB”) [24] and symmetric stabilizer benchmarking (“SSB”) [29]. (B) Return probability as a function of number of CZ gates, fitted to an exponential decay, giving a raw fidelity of 99.854(4)% and a loss- postselected fidelity of 99.941(3)%. (C) Comparison of CZ gate fidelity measured with different benchmarking protocols: echo RB with and without an extra single-qubit π pulse before readout, resonant pushout of |1⟩versus state-selective readout (SSR), and SSB versus echo RB. (D) Stability of the CZ fidelity measured for over 10 hours with...

FIG. 3. Error breakdown and path to further improve- ment. (A) Illustration of error sources for Rydberg entan- gling gates, including scattering from the intermediate state γe, Rydberg state lifetime γr, ground-Rydberg coherence T ∗ 2 , and coupling to the other Rydberg state |r′⟩= 53S1/2, mJ = +1/2. (B) Numerical error budget showing contributions of individual errors, total theoretical errors, and experimental data. Error breakdowns are shown for the current configu- ration, as well as projected values attainable with technical improvements and higher laser intensities [38].

FIG. 4. High-fidelity quantum circuits with atom mo- tion. (A) Raw and loss-postselected CZ fidelity measured with echo RB, with one atom in a static trap generated by a spatial light modulator (SLM) and one atom in a moving trap generated by an acousto-optic deflector (AOD). (B) Protocol for making and unmaking one-dimensional cluster states to study CZ gate performance in a quantum circuit. (C) Decay of global return probability Preturn as a function of the num- ber of total CZ gates, fit to an exponential decay, for N = 20 qubits. (D) Stability of the fitted decay over time, without recalibration.

FIG. 5. High-fidelity scrambling circuits with nonlocal connectivity. (A) Nonlocal quantum circuit implementation. CZ gates of increasing nonlocal connectivity (with labels given below the circuit) are interspersed with single-qubit gates, R = X(π/4). The resulting connectivity for the 20-qubit circuit is illustrated on the right. (B) Theoretical values for the half-system-size von Neumann entanglement entropy and XEBideal as a function of the degree of nonlocal CZ gates added to the circuit (“nonlocal connectivity”). Dashed lines indicate the Page entropy value and XEBideal = 1. (C) Loss per qubit per CZ gate compared for different circuits in this work: randomized benchmarking (RB), the...
